surviving.title: stalker.ts: fetchShortEpg is dead (unwired EPG fallback) and resetStalkerSession is dead with a stale doc comment claiming tests use it
surviving.evidence: grep -rl -w for each symbol across all src TS/TSX (including tests) and scripts/services returns only stalker.ts itself; internal occurrence count is 1 each (declaration only; the verify-script hits are Playwright getByPlaceholder, unrelated). fetchShortEpg's comment calls it 'the lazy fallback when a portal ignores get_epg_info', but source.ts:566 handles that case by setting epgError ('the portal returned no guide data (get_epg_info empty)') — no fallback path calls it. resetStalkerSession's comment (line 58) says 'tests, or a Settings credential edit' — no test imports it (stalker.test.ts included) and PlaylistsTab never calls it, so edited Stalker credentials keep serving the stale cached session token until it expires. Note: this is not on the documented intentional-dormant list (that covers mpv_blur/mpv_snapshot and stream.ts catch-up/timeshift only).
surviving.proposed_fix: Either wire them (call resetStalkerSession from the Settings credential-edit path — arguably a real gap since sessions cache ~1h — and fetchShortEpg as the documented EPG fallback) or delete both and their comments. Ask before deleting fetchShortEpg in case it is undocumented groundwork like the stream.ts catch-up code.

verdict.reason: The dead-code facts are accurate: repo-wide search confirms fetchShortEpg (stalker.ts:395) and resetStalkerSession (stalker.ts:59) are declaration-only, no dynamic use, no test imports (stalker.test.ts resets sessions via vi.resetModules(), line 113), and no fallback path calls fetchShortEpg (source.ts sets epgError instead). But the medium impact rested on a functional gap that does not exist: PlaylistsTab has NO credential-edit path — only add/delete/toggle/folder-edit — and a delete+re-add mints a fresh crypto.randomUUID() playlist id (playlists.ts:148), which keys the sessions map, so a stale cached session can never be served for changed credentials. Additionally, fetchShortEpg is documented groundwork: docs/stalker-implementation.md's "Recommended EPG strategy" explicitly plans get_short_epg as the lazy fallback, and ROADMAP.md:490 notes Stalker is "NOT yet proven against a real portal", so deletion would likely be wrong. What remains is a low-severity cleanliness issue: two unused exports and a comment naming callers (tests, a Settings credential edit) that never existed.

verdict.reason: Verified: no shero-related ::after exists anywhere in apps/app/src (grep confirms only episode-card__thumb::after and continue-card__artwrap::after), yet the comment at stream.css:122-123 claims overflow:hidden "would clip the ::after glow." The glow is in fact the sibling .shero__glowbox/.shero__glowtrack layer (stream.css:65-95, rendered in StreamScreen.tsx:1508-1525), which is outside .shero__card's subtree, so card overflow could never clip it — the rationale is mechanically false as written. git log -S shows a shero ::after never existed in the file's history; the glowbox was already present when the comment first appeared (e9cbe88). Additional corroboration: the same stale "::after glow" phrase also appears in a JSX comment at StreamScreen.tsx:1576 and should be fixed alongside. surviving.title: Unnecessary export keywords on ~20 symbols used only inside their own file
surviving.evidence: A sweep of every 'export (function|const|type|interface)' declaration vs whole-src usage (grep -rl -w per symbol, excluding the defining file, tests counted separately) found these exported but imported nowhere, not even by tests, while being used internally: stream/source.ts buildVod (:145) and pickEven (:365); stream/mapper.ts mapStream (:27); live/epg.ts windowEnd (:17); live/mock.ts programmesFor (:114), MOCK_FOLDERS (:15), MOCK_PLAYLIST_NAME (:11); app/BootScene.tsx LAND_MS (:36), SETTLE_MS (:38); settings/accent.ts AURORA_HUE (:63); settings/rowCap.ts clampRowCap (:14); settings/license.ts DEFAULT_KEYBOX (:38), keyboxUrl (:45); data/xtream.ts liveStreamsUrl, xmltvUrl; data/stalker.ts actionUrl; discover/data.ts genreForCatalog (:210); plus internally-used-only types StalkerChannel/StalkerGenre/StalkerProgramme, stremio.ts AddonManifest/CatalogResponse/MetaResponse/StreamResponse, model.ts LiveFolder, diskCache.ts DiskCached, m3u.ts M3uEntry, openRequest.ts OpenOrigin, playbackPrefs.ts PlaybackPrefs, license.ts Entitlement/ActivateResult, TheaterOverlay.tsx OverlayFrame, discover/data.ts DiscoverCatalog/GridSeed, http.ts HttpForensics, tauri.ts MpvStatus. Deliberately NOT flagged: test-only exports (a used pattern here, e.g. epgIndex, catchupStreamUrl) and the documented dormant tauriMpvBlur/tauriMpvSnapshot/timeshift surface.
surviving.proposed_fix: Drop the export keyword on the value symbols (buildVod, pickEven, mapStream, windowEnd, programmesFor, LAND_MS, SETTLE_MS, AURORA_HUE, clampRowCap, DEFAULT_KEYBOX, keyboxUrl, genreForCatalog, actionUrl, liveStreamsUrl, xmltvUrl, MOCK_FOLDERS, MOCK_PLAYLIST_NAME) so dead-code tooling and readers see the real module surface. The type exports are cheaper to leave (types describing returned shapes are legitimate API surface) — deprioritize or skip those.

surviving.title: public/logo.png is 722 KB (1143x1143 RGBA) but rendered at 35x28 px — 31% of the entire dist
surviving.evidence: public/logo.png = 721,762 bytes, PNG 1143x1143 8-bit RGBA (verified with file/ls). Its only uses: favicon (`` in apps/app/index.html:6) and `` in apps/app/src/app/AppHeader.tsx:178, styled at `width:35px; height:28px` (apps/app/src/styles/base.css:261-264). dist total is 2.3 MB, so this one file is ~31% of the shipped web assets. Meanwhile apps/app/public/logo.svg (4,637 bytes) is copied into dist but referenced nowhere in src, index.html, or tauri.conf.json (grep found zero references).
surviving.proposed_fix: Point both the favicon and .header__logo at the existing logo.svg (4.6 KB), or export a small PNG (~70x56 for 2x DPI, a few KB) if raster is preferred for the favicon. Then delete whichever of logo.png/logo.svg is left unreferenced. Saves ~700 KB (~30% of dist).

verdict.reason: All raw facts verified: logo.png is 721,762 bytes at 1143x1143 RGBA, used only as favicon (index.html:6) and header img (AppHeader.tsx:178) rendered 35x28 (base.css:261-265); it is ~33% of dist; fake-panel/fake-m3u serve an inline 1x1 PNG, not this file; ROADMAP documents the brand mark's identity, not its file weight, so an SVG swap is compatible with documented intent. But impact is overstated two ways. (1) Wrong denominator: this is a Tauri desktop app — dist is embedded in the ~38.9 MB exe (HANDOFF.md v0.4.43), so the saving is ~1.8% of the artifact users download, with zero network/page-load path; the only runtime cost is decoding a 1143x1143 bitmap for a 35x28 image. (2) Evidence error: logo.svg is NOT unreferenced repo-wide — website/build-landing.mjs:16 inlines apps/app/public/logo.svg into the landing page, so the fix's "delete whichever is left unreferenced" step could break the website build if the small-PNG route were taken. Real, easy debloat win (point both refs at logo.svg, delete logo.png, keep logo.svg), but low user-visible impact, not high.

surviving.title: 528 KB of .woff font fallbacks shipped that WebView2 can never download
surviving.evidence: dist/assets contains 70 font files totaling 996 KB: 35 .woff2 (468 KB) + 35 .woff (528 KB). The built CSS (dist/assets/index-BXJVhaWK.css) has 41 @font-face rules, each `src: url(...woff2) format("woff2"), url(...woff) format("woff")` — the Fontsource default. Chromium/WebView2 (the only target; Linux is just the sandbox) always resolves the woff2 source, so every .woff file is unreachable dead weight in the installer. That is 23% of dist and 53% of all font bytes.

Keeps the documented offline/no-CDN design (fonts.ts header comment) fully intact — same families, same weights, half the bytes.

verdict.reason: Every number re-verified against the real dist: 35 .woff2 (468K) + 35 .woff (528K), 41 @font-face rules each listing woff2 first, so Chromium/WebView2 (and even the WebKitGTK sandbox) never fetch the .woff sources — 528KB (23% of the 2.3M dist) is provably dead. Not intentional: fonts.ts's own header says only woff2 files are bundled and the goal is a lean bundle, so the woff duplicates are an unnoticed Fontsource-default side effect; ROADMAP/HANDOFF/CLAUDE never mention font formats. The only .woff consumer in the repo (website/build-landing.mjs) reads from node_modules, not dist, so neither proposed fix breaks it. One caveat for the fixer: the 35 files are unicode-range subsets (latin, hebrew, etc.); a hand-written fonts.css must keep all subsets — the generateBundle-hook variant is subset-safe by construction. Medium impact stands.

surviving.title: z-index ladder exists only in scattered comments, with outliers that break it
surviving.evidence: Values span -2, -1, 0..5, 20 (base.css:126 header), 30 (stream.css:1226 popped stage), 40 (live.css:387 .live-tip), 45 (player.css:248 #inv-chrome, stream.css:1153), 46 (stream.css:1163, player.css:833), 50 (stream.css:931), 60 (settings.css:10), 70 (settings.css:840), 200 (live.css:906 .live--fullscreen .hero__preview), 1000 (boot.css:59, onboarding.css:14). The ladder is documented only via cross-file prose (settings.css:8 'the theater backdrop (40)' — a value that exists nowhere in CSS except .live-tip; player.css:245 'Above the theater backdrop (40), below the settings modal (60)'). live.css:906's 200 is an unexplained outlier that also sits ABOVE the settings modal (60) and chip-select menu (70), so a modal opened over fullscreen live paints under the preview box.
surviving.proposed_fix: Add a --z-* scale to tokens.css (e.g. --z-header:20, --z-popped:30, --z-tip:40, --z-player-chrome:45, --z-player-float:46, --z-upnext:50, --z-modal:60, --z-menu:70, --z-boot:1000) and reference it everywhere; either fold the fullscreen 200 into the scale deliberately (documenting why it must beat modals) or lower it to the chrome tier.

verdict.reason: The inventory is accurate (scattered magic z-indexes, no --z-* tokens, prose-only ladder) and the prose has genuinely drifted — settings.css:8 and player.css:244 cite a "theater backdrop (40)" that no longer exists (the only 40 is .live-tip; .theater-overlay has no z-index). But the headline harm is refuted: the fullscreen preview's z-index:200 (live.css:906) cannot paint over the settings modal. .app-shell has isolation:isolate (base.css:74), trapping the 200 inside the shell's stacking context (root-level z auto), while SettingsModal is portaled to document.body (SettingsModal.tsx:38-43, with a comment documenting exactly this architecture) where its 60 beats the shell's auto — the modal always paints above. During playback the shell's clip-path video hole clips the preview box away entirely, and on the composition/native path ROADMAP.md v0.1.114 documents that no CSS z-index matters at all (data-native-hidden parks the native layer). The 200 only needs to beat in-shell chrome (header 20, live-tip 40) for the pre-video black slate, which is coherent. What remains is a low-impact cleanliness/doc-drift issue; note any tokenized fix must model TWO ladders (in-shell vs body-level portals) — the finder's flat scale conflates stacking contexts.

surviving.title: Supporter's !important cluster contradicts the 'one sanctioned !important' doctrine; streamy proves it unnecessary
surviving.evidence: tokens.css:93-94 declares the sharp-corner radius zero 'the one sanctioned !important'. Supporter uses six more: `background: transparent !important` (199), `background: #00000040 !important` (208), the --live cell (221), `display: none !important` (229, 236), `padding-right: 0 !important` (246). Streamy overrides the identical occluder surfaces at 487-492 with NO !important — the `:root[data-theme-pack]` prefix already gives (0,3,0) specificity, beating every (0,1,0)/(0,2,0) rule in the later-loaded feature sheets, and Guide.tsx sets no inline backgrounds. The only arguably load-bearing one is line 221: ui.css:276's `:root[data-accent-style="aurora"] .guide__cell--live` is equal specificity and loads later, so supporter+aurora needs the weight — but that isn't what the adjacent comment says (it claims source-order vs the generic cell rule).
surviving.proposed_fix: Drop the !importants at 199, 208, 229, 236, 246; keep 221 but rewrite its comment to name the real reason (beating ui.css's later-loaded equal-specificity aurora rule), or bump its specificity instead.

verdict.reason: Every element checks out. tokens.css:93-94 declares the sharp-corner rule "the one sanctioned !important", yet supporter carries six more at exactly the cited lines (198, 208, 221, 228, 235, 246) — the only other !importants in all 13 stylesheets. No ROADMAP/HANDOFF/CLAUDE documentation sanctions these weights, and no WebView2 workaround is implicated (the block's WebView2 note concerns backdrop-filter). surviving.title: Card (Tilt-wrapped) mapped in every big grid without memo; unstable metaFields identity would defeat memo where it matters most
surviving.evidence: Card (StreamScreen.tsx:1675) is a plain function component wrapping react-parallax-tilt (a stateful pointer-tracking component) and is mapped in three large lists: Stream home rows (StreamScreen.tsx:1265-1282, rowCap default 40 items/row across many rows = 200-400 mounted cards), the Discover infinite-scroll grid (DiscoverScreen.tsx:413-420, unbounded growth), and My List (MyListScreen.tsx:68-74). Only Guide is memoized in the whole app (grep for memo( returns one hit). Concrete re-render storms: (a) every keystroke in the header search calls setSearchQuery (AppHeader.tsx:299) which fires DiscoverScreen's setQuery subscriber (DiscoverScreen.tsx:90) — the entire mounted grid of Cards re-renders per keystroke; (b) every infinite-scroll append (setItems at DiscoverScreen.tsx:256) re-renders all previously loaded cards; (c) each genre-art callback setArt (DiscoverScreen.tsx:170-175, one per genre as resolveGenreArt streams in) re-renders the grid; (d) on Stream home, enrichFeatured calls notifyUpdate once PER featured item (source.ts:210-222), each producing setLoad({...data}) (StreamScreen.tsx:194-198) → Home re-renders every Card in every row N times shortly after load. Card props are already memo-friendly on Stream home (items map mutated in place so item identity is stable; cardOpen/open are stable useCallbacks; metaFields is state) — but Discover's gridMetaFields is rebuilt as a fresh array every render in the DEFAULT filter ('all'): `[...metaFields, "kind"]` (DiscoverScreen.tsx:81-84), and MyListScreen rebuilds both toItem(e) objects and its gridMetaFields array per render (MyListScreen.tsx:44-46, 71), so memo alone would be defeated there.
surviving.proposed_fix: Wrap Card in React.memo. In DiscoverScreen, hoist gridMetaFields into useMemo([filter, metaFields]). In MyListScreen, useMemo the entries.map(toItem) array and the gridMetaFields array. Optionally memo ContinueCard too, giving Home stable per-entry callbacks (useCallback keyed maps or pass entry + id-taking handlers) since its onOpen/onSources/onClear are currently inline closures (StreamScreen.tsx:1257-1259).

verdict.reason: Mechanics nearly all verify: Card (StreamScreen.tsx:1675) is unmemoized, wraps Tilt, and is mapped in all three big lists; only Guide is memoized; the enrich storm (9 notifyUpdate passes, FEATURED_TOTAL=9 in source.ts:26, each a fresh setLoad object), the infinite-scroll append re-render (DiscoverScreen.tsx:256), the per-genre setArt passes (170-175), and the memo-defeating unstable identities (DiscoverScreen.tsx:81-84 fresh gridMetaFields in default 'all' filter; MyListScreen.tsx:44-46,71 fresh toItem objects/array) are all real, and the proposed fix is correct, safe, and contradicts no documented decision. But the headline storm (a) is materially overstated: at >=2 chars `searching` flips true and the browse grid UNMOUNTS (DiscoverScreen.tsx:332), with setResults(null) rendering only a "Searching…" note between keystrokes — so the full-grid re-render happens on 1-2 sub-2-char keystrokes per search, not every keystroke, and the mount/unmount churn memo can't help. The surviving storms are bounded one-shots (9 enrich passes at load, ~genre-count art passes per mount; MyList barely re-renders) except the infinite-scroll append, whose user-visible cost is inferred (no-DOM-change reconciliation, composited scrolling, 600px prefetch) rather than measured. Real and worth fixing, but medium, not high.

surviving.title: playMeta rebuilt every render feeds useDirectOverlay's [meta] effect — TheaterOverlay re-renders on every LiveScreen/StreamScreen render (worst: per guide hover-preview during playback)
surviving.evidence: LiveScreen builds playMeta in a render-time IIFE (LiveScreen.tsx:418-431) — new Date(), a .find() over the channel's programmes, and a fresh buildMeta object every render. useDirectOverlay's meta effect is keyed on object identity: `useEffect(() => { metaRef.current = meta; s.metaCbs.forEach(cb => cb(meta)); }, [meta, s])` (useDirectOverlay.ts:79-82), so it fires on EVERY host render, and TheaterOverlay's subscriber is setMeta (TheaterOverlay.tsx:168) — a guaranteed state update with a fresh object → full re-render of the 1288-line chrome, plus its downstream [meta] effect (setFav, TheaterOverlay.tsx:303-305). LiveScreen re-renders on every hover-preview (Guide's onMouseEnter → onPreview → setPreview, Guide.tsx:499/581 → LiveScreen.tsx:819) — the exact traffic Guide's own memo comment documents as 'constantly'. Net: sweeping the cursor across guide cells while the mini/theater player is up re-renders the whole player chrome once per cell crossing. StreamScreen has the identical pattern (playMeta built inline at StreamScreen.tsx:630-647, including a nextEpisode() scan of all seasons) and re-renders every 5s from the progress tick, pushing a fresh meta into TheaterOverlay each time.

surviving.title: LiveScreen sidebar (groups × folders, splitTitleEmoji per folder) rendered inline — re-renders on every hover-preview and tooltip state change

LiveScreen re-renders on every setPreview (each guide-cell mouseenter) and every setTip (folded-rail hover, LiveScreen.tsx:676). The project already spent effort protecting Guide from exactly this traffic (Guide.tsx:151-153 memo comment) and useConnections deliberately keeps its Map identity stable 'so the sidebar re-renders on every tick otherwise' (connections.ts:35-37) — but the sidebar still re-renders per hover anyway because it lives in LiveScreen's own render. surviving.title: VOD progress tick drives setWatching every 5s (1s while popped) — state churn re-rendering the playback stage while the only consumer (Home) is unmounted
surviving.evidence: The 5s progress interval calls setWatching(updateWatchingProgress(...)) (StreamScreen.tsx:832-838); updateWatchingProgress always returns a freshly-mapped array (watching.ts: `loadWatching().map(...)` + save), so every tick is a guaranteed new state identity → full StreamScreen re-render. During playback StreamScreen early-returns the vod-stage branch (StreamScreen.tsx:898), so Home — the only thing that renders `watching` — is unmounted; the re-render only churns the stage: the portaled 1288-line TheaterOverlay (not memoized), InvertedPlayer, panel/upnext portals, plus a fresh playMeta rebuild whose nextEpisode() scan and meta push (finding 2) fire again. While popped the same pattern runs at 1s cadence (StreamScreen.tsx:778-787). Resume/quick-resume paths all read loadWatching() from storage directly, not the state, so the state update serves nothing until playback ends.
surviving.proposed_fix: During playback, persist without setState: call updateWatchingProgress for its storage write but drop the setWatching (or add an updateWatchingProgressQuiet). Re-sync state once on stop/onEnded/onPopoutClosed with setWatching(loadWatching()). This removes the periodic re-render entirely; the storage write cadence (which powers resume) is unchanged.

verdict.reason: Every mechanical claim verifies: the 5s tick (StreamScreen.tsx:832-838) and popped 1s poll (778-787) call setWatching with a guaranteed-fresh array (watching.ts:69-75 always maps+saves), the only renderer of `watching` (Home, line 1120-1124) is unmounted behind the playback early-return at 898, all resume paths read loadWatching() from storage, TheaterOverlay (1288 lines) is un-memoized, and the playMeta rebuild (630-646) re-fires the useDirectOverlay meta push (useDirectOverlay.ts:79-82) each tick. Nothing in ROADMAP/HANDOFF/CLAUDE documents it as intentional, and the proposed fix is safe (state initializer at line 118 covers remounts). But medium overstates the cost: cadence is one render per 5s, TheaterOverlay already re-renders at ~2Hz from its own 500ms time-poll subscription during VOD, video is rendered natively by mpv so DOM churn can't affect playback, and the popped 1s case churns a near-empty tree (overlay and InvertedPlayer are both gated out by !popped). Real, correctly diagnosed, cheap to fix — but zero user-perceivable impact, so low, not medium.

surviving.title: TheaterOverlay runs document.elementFromPoint on every mousemove to feed setMouseIgnore — a documented no-op on the inline (shipping) path
surviving.evidence: The activity tracker's document-level mousemove handler does `document.elementFromPoint(e.clientX, e.clientY)` + `el.closest("[data-interactive]")` on every pointer move while theater/fullscreen chrome is mounted (TheaterOverlay.tsx:439-442), solely to call api()?.setMouseIgnore(ig). On the inline inverted-player path — the only shipping path; the overlay-webview is a kept-for-tests leftover per the component's own header comment — setMouseIgnore is literally `() => {}` ('real DOM above the video — clicks just work', useDirectOverlay.ts:187). elementFromPoint forces a hit-test (and a layout flush when style/layout is dirty) per mousemove during playback, all to compute a discarded boolean.
surviving.proposed_fix: Gate the hit-test on the path that needs it: inline mode is identifiable by the `frame` prop being set (TheaterOverlay.tsx:153 already branches on it). In the mousemove handler, when `frame` is present skip elementFromPoint/closest and only call wake(). The overlay-webview fallback keeps the current behavior.

verdict.reason: Every element of the claim checks out on re-inspection. TheaterOverlay.tsx:439-443 runs document.elementFromPoint + closest("[data-interactive]") on every mousemove in theater/fullscreen, solely to feed setMouseIgnore, which is a literal no-op (() => {}) in the app's only OverlayApi implementation (useDirectOverlay.ts:187). This is not documented intent to keep — ROADMAP.md:441 explicitly lists setMouseIgnore among the things the v0.2.0 layer inversion eliminated, so the hit-test is a leftover of the deleted comp.rs overlay webview. No dynamic dependency exists: all four verify-*.mjs harness scripts stub setMouseIgnore as a no-op and never assert on it. The proposed fix's premise holds — both shipping mounts always pass `frame` (LiveScreen.tsx:793 always mini/theater/fullscreen; StreamScreen.tsx:906 with mode typed "theater"|"fullscreen"), and the frame-less main.tsx `?overlay=1` harness route keeps current behavior. No WebView2 workaround is affected. Impact is correctly rated low: the setMouseIgnore call itself is already deduped by the `ignoring` guard, mousemove is frame-coalesced, and elementFromPoint forces a layout flush only when layout is dirty — wasted work, but small.

surviving.title: onTime pushes every 500ms re-render the entire theater chrome for a clock/scrubber that lives in one corner of it
surviving.evidence: useDirectOverlay's 500ms mpv_status poll pushes a fresh TimeInfo whenever dur > 0 (useDirectOverlay.ts:143-146); TheaterOverlay subscribes with setTime at the top level (TheaterOverlay.tsx:192-195), so during any VOD playback the whole ~450-line theater JSX tree (top buttons, meta block, track menus, controls, skip-chip computation including two chapter scans at lines 569-642) re-renders twice a second for the lifetime of the stream — even when the chrome is faded out (active=false only changes a class; the tree stays mounted). The skip/credits scans over chapters + meta.skips also re-execute on each of those renders.
surviving.proposed_fix: Move the time subscription down: extract the seek row + time labels (and the skip-chip/credits computation, which are the only time consumers) into a small child component that subscribes to onTime itself. The parent chrome then re-renders only on real state changes (pause, volume, menu, tracks). Low urgency — the tree is cheap to diff — but it compounds with findings 2 and 4 during playback.

verdict.reason: All mechanics verified against source. useDirectOverlay.ts:143-146 pushes a brand-new {pos,dur} object to timeCbs on every 500ms poll tick whenever dur > 0, with no dedupe (tracks and chapters ARE JSON-deduped; time is not — so renders fire even while paused with pos unchanged). TheaterOverlay.tsx:192-195 subscribes with the component's top-level setTime, so the entire theater/fullscreen return (lines 791-1207, including the skip-chip/credits chapter scans at 568-642) re-renders twice a second for the stream's lifetime; active=false only toggles a class (794-798), the tree stays mounted. No documented intent covers this: ROADMAP.md documents the 500ms poll as deliberate, but the proposed fix (narrow the subscription to a child) keeps the poll and breaks no WebView2 workaround; nothing on the scars list applies. Claimed impact "low" is correctly calibrated — React diff of this tree is cheap and while playing the time labels/fill legitimately need updating — so the finding holds exactly as stated.

surviving.title: window.matchMedia evaluated on every render via useRef initializer argument in Card and the Stream Hero
surviving.evidence: Card does `useRef(window.matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches).current` (StreamScreen.tsx:1701-1703) and Hero the same (StreamScreen.tsx:1482-1484). useRef's argument is evaluated on every render even though only the first value is kept — so every render of every Card performs a matchMedia call. Today that is hundreds of calls per keystroke in the Discover grid (finding 1's storm); after Card is memoized it still runs once per mounted card and per Hero render (Hero re-renders every 8s on the carousel tick and on each enrichment repaint).
surviving.proposed_fix: Hoist to a module-level constant (`const REDUCED_MOTION = window.matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches`) or a tiny shared hook with a lazy useState initializer (`useState(() => ...)`), and reuse it in both components. Matches the existing pattern of module constants like NO_PROGRAMMES.

verdict.reason: Verified both call sites (StreamScreen.tsx:1482-1484 Hero, 1701-1703 Card): useRef's argument is evaluated every render, so window.matchMedia runs per Card/Hero render. Card is unmemoized (line 1675) and rendered per-item in the Discover grid (DiscoverScreen.tsx:342,414); Hero re-renders on its 8s carousel tick (lines 1471-1475). Nearby comments and the parallel Onboarding.tsx:157-161 pattern document only the fixed-at-mount semantics, which the proposed module-constant/lazy-useState fix preserves — no documented intent, scar-list entry, or WebView2 workaround is contradicted. surviving.title: Stream Home renders a completely blank page when the catalog loads empty
surviving.evidence: Home has branches for unconfigured (line 1213), loading (1224), and error (1228), but the ready path renders `{featured.length > 0 && <Hero…>}` plus `data.rows.map(...)` with no empty branch. buildVod (features/stream/source.ts:161-163) swallows per-catalog failures into `{ cat, items: [] }` and rows with 0 items are skipped (source.ts:174), so a manifest that loads while every catalog page fails yields `rows: [], featured: []` and NO `error` field — a silent blank tab. Discover shows "Nothing here — the catalog returned no titles." (DiscoverScreen.tsx:406-410) and Live shows "No channels here yet…" (LiveScreen.tsx:763-765) for the equivalent state.
surviving.proposed_fix: In Home, when `load.status === "ready"` and `featured.length === 0 && data.rows.every(r => r.itemIds.length === 0) && activeWatching.length === 0`, render a `.stream__note` in the family voice, e.g. "Nothing came back from your catalogs — check the manifest in Settings → AIOStreams." Optionally have buildVod set `error` when every catalog fetch failed so the real error state shows instead.

verdict.reason: Mechanism fully confirmed: Home (StreamScreen.tsx:1213-1285) has unconfigured/loading/error branches but no empty branch; buildVod (source.ts:161-163, 174) swallows per-catalog failures and skips empty rows, defaultHero([]) and buildFeatured([]) return [] without throwing, so an all-catalogs-failed (or zero-browseable-catalogs) manifest yields {rows: [], featured: []} with no error field and the load reducer lands on status "ready" — the tab content renders as an empty div. Sibling screens (Discover 408, Live 764, MyList 55) all have empty states; no documented intent in ROADMAP.md/HANDOFF.md excuses the gap. Impact downgraded from high to medium: it is the tab content, not the whole page, that goes blank (app chrome/Settings remain reachable); the disk/memory cache only pins non-empty builds and stale-while-revalidate never replaces a shown catalog with a failure, so only first-run/fresh-config users with all catalogs failing or a catalogs-less manifest — and no Continue Watching entries — ever see it. Real, worth fixing exactly as proposed, but the fully-blank scenario is a narrow intersection rather than a broadly-hit failure.

surviving.title: Error states dead-end on Stream and Discover while Live gets a Try-again button
surviving.evidence: LiveScreen's two error states both render a retry (`live-status__retry` "Try again" → refresh(false), LiveScreen.tsx:736-742 and 754-760). The sibling surfaces have none: Discover's "Couldn't load Discover." (DiscoverScreen.tsx:312-317) offers only the message; Stream Home's "Couldn't load your catalog." (StreamScreen.tsx:1228-1234) likewise; the detail rail's "Couldn't load sources." (StreamScreen.tsx:2037-2039) and the in-playback panel's version (939-941) also have no retry — the only recovery is navigating away and back, which nothing tells the user.
surviving.proposed_fix: Add a retry button to each: Discover re-runs the loadDiscover effect (lift it into a callback like Live's refresh); Stream Home re-calls loadVod(true); the sources rail/panel re-runs resolveVodSources. Reuse the live-status__retry / btn-primary styling so the affordance reads the same across tabs.

verdict.reason: All five citations verified verbatim: Live's two error states have live-status__retry "Try again" (LiveScreen.tsx:736-742, 754-760) while Discover (DiscoverScreen.tsx:312-317), Stream Home (StreamScreen.tsx:1228-1234), the detail sources rail (2037-2039), and the in-playback panel (939-941) have no retry. No documented intent — ROADMAP's only "Retry" mention is the unrelated tune-watchdog card. The finding is even slightly understated in one way: the error text directs users to fix Settings, but SettingsModal is an overlay and neither screen re-runs its mount-only load effect on config change, so fixing the URL doesn't clear the error until a tab switch. However, "high" overstates exposure: StreamScreen's stale-while-revalidate (a failed refresh never replaces a shown catalog, StreamScreen.tsx:206-210) plus disk cache means the error screen appears essentially only on first-run/misconfig; recovery exists via tab switch since screens unmount on section change (App.tsx:146-154) and failures aren't cached (source.ts:120-124); and the sources states recover by reopening the detail/panel. Real cross-tab affordance inconsistency on rare error paths — medium for a polish audit.

surviving.title: Discover renders fetch failures as authoritative empty results
surviving.evidence: Search: `searchDiscover(...).then(r => setResults(r), () => setResults([]))` (line 133) — a network failure renders "No results for “{q}”." (line 338), a definitive claim the app can't back. Browse grid: every page fetch failure maps to null (line 234), all-null leaves `items` empty with phase "idle", which renders "Nothing here — the catalog returned no titles" (406-410) — again stating the catalog is empty when the catalog was never heard from. (The null-page comment at 231-234 documents cursor bookkeeping, not this message, so it doesn't sanction the copy.)
surviving.proposed_fix: Track failure distinctly: search error → a `results: "failed"` state rendering "Search didn't go through — try again." with retry; grid reset where ALL pages returned null → an error note (with retry) instead of the empty-catalog copy.

verdict.reason: Both false-empty states are real and undocumented. Grid: all-pages-failed reset leaves items=[] with phase "idle", rendering "Nothing here — the catalog returned no titles" (DiscoverScreen.tsx:234, 239, 257-260, 406-410); the null-page comment covers only cursor bookkeeping, and no ROADMAP/HANDOFF/CLAUDE.md entry sanctions the copy. Search: the behavior holds but the finder's mechanism is wrong — searchDiscover catches every per-catalog failure internally (data.ts:172) and resolves with [], so the rejection handler at DiscoverScreen.tsx:133 is effectively dead code and the false "No results for {q}" arrives via the resolved path. That makes the problem slightly worse than claimed (even one failing catalog silently drops its results), but it means the proposed search fix (map the outer rejection to a "failed" state) would never fire; the fix must track failures inside searchDiscover instead. Impact stays medium: definitive false copy on network failure, tempered by requiring the failure to occur after config loaded (cold-start failures hit the existing error screen).

Search: the behavior holds but the finder's mechanism is wrong — searchDiscover catches every per-catalog failure internally (data.ts:172) and resolves with [], so the rejection handler at DiscoverScreen.tsx:133 is effectively dead code and the false "No results for {q}" arrives via the resolved path. That makes the problem slightly worse than claimed (even one failing catalog silently drops its results), but it means the proposed search fix (map the outer rejection to a "failed" state) would never fire; the fix must track failures inside searchDiscover instead. surviving.title: Themed :focus-visible ring exists only on Live (and Onboarding); Stream/Discover/My List/header get the browser default
surviving.evidence: live.css:1055-1072 declares "One themed ring for every interactive Live control" (mode rail, groups, folders, cards, cells, retry, resize). Grep across styles finds no :focus-visible rule in stream.css, discover.css, ui.css or base.css — stream cards, continue cards, hero carousel, genre cards, ChipTabs, header actions, vod-source rows, season/episode chips all fall back to the Chromium default ring. ROADMAP.md:534-541 records the a11y batch as a Live-only pass (v0.1.98), so the sibling tabs were simply never covered; the tabs feel like two different apps under keyboard use.
surviving.proposed_fix: Extend the same ring: one grouped rule per sheet (or a shared selector in ui.css) applying `outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px` on :focus-visible to .stream-card, .continue-card, .shero__card, .genre-card, .chip-tabs__tab, .header__tab, .header__action, .vod-source, .vod-back, .season-chip, .episode-card, .media-row__arrow, .btn-primary and settings controls.

verdict.reason: Every factual element re-verified: live.css:1055-1072 declares the themed accent ring for Live controls only; a fresh grep of all 13 stylesheets finds :focus-visible rules solely in live.css and onboarding.css — none in stream.css, discover.css, ui.css, base.css, or settings.css. The affected Stream/Discover/header controls are genuinely keyboard-focusable (stream-card is a <button>, continue-card is role=button tabIndex=0, shero__card has roving tabIndex, header tabs/actions and ChipTabs are buttons), and no global outline:none or JS focus-ring mechanism covers them, so they truly fall back to the unthemed Chromium default ring. ROADMAP.md:533-541 confirms the v0.1.98 batch was scoped as a Live-tab pass — it documents scope, not a decision to exclude sibling tabs, so this is a real coverage gap rather than documented intent. Fix is plain CSS and touches no WebView2 scar. Medium impact holds: the project's own standard (Playwright-verified Live a11y pass) makes the cross-tab inconsistency a legitimate polish defect, while a functional default ring everywhere keeps it below high.

surviving.title: Continue Watching card: no keyboard path to clear, Space scrolls the page, Sources chip unreachable by keyboard
surviving.evidence: The role=button div (1786-1798) only maps Enter/Space to onOpen; onClear is reachable solely via a 1s pointer hold (1771-1779) — keyboard users cannot remove an entry. The Space keydown calls onOpen without preventDefault, so the scroll container also pages down. The "Sources ›" chip is opacity:0 until :hover (stream.css:1101-1104) with no focus reveal — contrast Live's identical pattern, where the hover-revealed star gets `.guide__channel:focus-within .guide__fav` (live.css:678-679).
surviving.proposed_fix: e.preventDefault() on Space; add a Delete/Backspace handler (or a focus-revealed ✕ button) calling onClear; add `.continue-card:focus-within .continue-card__sources { opacity: 1; }` mirroring the guide-star rule.

verdict.reason: All three defects verified in code: (1) StreamScreen.tsx:1796-1798 maps Space to onOpen on a div role=button with no preventDefault, so the scroll default fires too; (2) onClear is bound only to the pointer-hold timer (1771-1779 via onPointerDown at 1790) — no Delete/Backspace handler exists anywhere in features/stream, so keyboard users cannot clear an entry; (3) the Sources chip is a real <button> (so technically tab-reachable, the one overstated word in the title) but stream.css contains zero focus rules — the only reveal is .continue-card:hover (stream.css:1104), leaving the focused chip invisible; worse, Enter on the focused chip bubbles keydown to the parent, firing both onSources and onOpen. Fix breaks no documented workaround. Medium impact stands.

surviving.title: ChipTabs carries no selected-state semantics while Live's equivalent rail is a full WAI-ARIA tablist
surviving.evidence: ChipTabs buttons (77-90) expose no aria-selected/aria-pressed/aria-current and no roving tabindex — the active chip differs only by opacity. verdict.reason: Every cited line checks out: ChipTabs buttons (ui/ChipTabs.tsx:77-90) expose no role/aria-selected/aria-pressed/roving tabindex and the active state is only opacity (styles/ui.css:28-31) with the thumb aria-hidden; ModeRail (LiveScreen.tsx:87-103,137-186) is a full WAI-ARIA tablist; season chips (StreamScreen.tsx:2184-2197) lack pressed state while genre-card (DiscoverScreen.tsx:366) sets aria-pressed. surviving.title: Quick-resume failure is a silent no-op; episodes screen shows "Loading episodes…" forever on meta failure
surviving.evidence: quickResume: `if (!item) { setResolving(null); return; }` (512-515) — when resolveVodItem fails, a Continue Watching click flashes the resolving screen then lands back on Home with zero feedback (watchNow, by contrast, falls back to the detail page). Related: Episodes renders "Loading episodes…" whenever `item.seasons.length === 0` (2179-2180); open()'s resolveVodItem catch keeps the light item (295-297), so a failed or season-less series meta shows that loading line permanently — an error state dressed as eternal progress.
surviving.proposed_fix: quickResume: on item-resolve failure show a transient error note (or route to the detail page with the light entry data). Episodes: track whether the full meta resolve settled; render "Couldn't load episodes — try again" (with retry) or "No episodes listed" instead of the loading line once it has.

verdict.reason: Both claims verified in apps/app/src/features/stream/StreamScreen.tsx. (1) quickResume: lines 512-515 silently return after resolveVodItem fails (catch → null at 505) when the item isn't in the loaded catalog map (sole caller at 1129 passes load.data.items.get(e.id)); user sees the resolving screen flash then lands back on Home with zero feedback. The function's own comment (493-495, "Any miss falls back to the detail/source screen") is contradicted by this branch — the source-miss and source-error paths fall back to open(item) at 563, but item-resolve failure does not, so it is not deliberate. (2) Episodes: lines 2179-2180 render "Loading episodes…" purely on item.seasons.length === 0 with no resolve-settled signal in the component's props (2098-2109), and open()'s catch (295-297) keeps the light item, so a failed or genuinely season-less series meta shows the loading line permanently. No documented intent in ROADMAP.md/HANDOFF.md/CLAUDE.md covers either behavior; not on the scars list; no WebView2 workaround implicated. Failure-path-only (addon down/offline/item aged out of catalog) but the failure is silent or misleading when hit — medium impact stands.

surviving.title: Deleting a playlist is one-click destructive; the app's own Danger Zone requires arm-then-confirm for the same class of action
surviving.evidence: The ✕ on a playlist row calls `update(removePlaylist(playlists, p.id))` immediately (239-246) — stored credentials (server/username/password or portal/MAC) are gone in one click with no undo, and hiddenCategories curation with them. CustomizeTab's "Clear All Login Info" (152-177) treats exactly this data as needing a two-click arm/confirm ("Clearing credentials is destructive, so it takes two clicks"). The two surfaces contradict each other.
surviving.proposed_fix: Reuse the arm pattern on the row delete: first click arms (button swaps to "Click again to remove", 4s timeout), second click deletes — same mechanics as clearLogins so the behavior language stays consistent.

verdict.reason: Verified in source: PlaylistsTab.tsx:239-246 deletes and persists (savePlaylists via update()) on a single click of the row ✕ with no arm, confirm, or undo, while CustomizeTab.tsx:152-177 arms-then-confirms (4s timeout, "Click again to confirm") for clearing the same data — its own comment says "Clearing credentials is destructive, so it takes two clicks" and its action includes savePlaylists([]). No documented intent in ROADMAP.md, HANDOFF.md, or CLAUDE.md covers one-click playlist deletion; it is not on the scars list and is not a WebView2 workaround. The loss includes credentials and hiddenCategories curation (which FolderEditor elsewhere protects with staged Save/Discard), and the ✕ sits adjacent to the toggle and expand chevron, making misclicks plausible. Medium impact holds: recoverable-with-effort data loss plus a genuine internal inconsistency in the app's destructive-action language.

surviving.title: Stream Home error path skips the credential scrubber Discover uses for the same failure
surviving.evidence: DiscoverScreen wraps its load rejection in scrubbedMessage with the comment "transport errors echo the FULL manifest URL (the credential) and this string renders on screen" (DiscoverScreen.tsx:147-154). StreamScreen's parallel rejection handler renders `e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e)` raw (213-221 → shown at 1232). Today loadVod's doLoad catches and scrubs internally (source.ts:138-140), so the raw path is mostly defensive — but it's the one branch of this fetch family not routed through lib/errors.ts, and the Connection Test explicitly promises "it never shows your URL" (AioStreamsTab.tsx:304).
surviving.proposed_fix: Use scrubbedMessage(e) in StreamScreen's loadVod rejection handler, matching Discover.

verdict.reason: All cited evidence verified: DiscoverScreen.tsx:147-154 scrubs its parallel rejection, StreamScreen.tsx:213-221 renders the raw message at line 1232, source.ts doLoad scrubs internally so the fulfilled {error} branch is safe, and AioStreamsTab.tsx:304 makes the "never shows your URL" promise. Deeper probe: loadVod effectively cannot reject with a credential-bearing message today (lib/storage.ts swallows all storage exceptions; buildVod failures are caught and scrubbed in doLoad), so the title's "same failure" framing is loose — Discover's scrub is load-bearing while Stream's is purely defensive, which the finder's own evidence concedes. It still stands because lib/errors.ts documents "anything user-facing or logged must pass through here first" and this is the one user-facing branch in the fetch family that doesn't; HANDOFF.md shows URL scrubbing was a deliberate cross-source security fix (v0.1.134 finding 3), so the gap contradicts documented intent rather than being covered by it; and the safety currently depends on doLoad's catch placement, which a refactor could silently break with the render at 1232 as the leak point. The one-line scrubbedMessage fix is behavior-preserving for all reachable paths. Claimed impact "low" is accurate — no reachable leak today, real convention violation and hardening value.

It still stands because lib/errors.ts documents "anything user-facing or logged must pass through here first" and this is the one user-facing branch in the fetch family that doesn't; HANDOFF.md shows URL scrubbing was a deliberate cross-source security fix (v0.1.134 finding 3), so the gap contradicts documented intent rather than being covered by it; and the safety currently depends on doLoad's catch placement, which a refactor could silently break with the render at 1232 as the leak point. The one-line scrubbedMessage fix is behavior-preserving for all reachable paths. Claimed impact "low" is accurate — no reachable leak today, real convention violation and hardening value.

surviving.title: Race-sensitive popout + #inv-chrome wiring duplicated (and already diverging) between LiveScreen and StreamScreen
surviving.evidence: The documented 'heal the shell's clip hole BEFORE Rust tears the video child down' sequence (querySelector('.app-shell') → clipPath='' → tauriPopoutOpen) exists verbatim in two places: LiveScreen.tsx:454-466 and StreamScreen.tsx:664-673 — both carry the same scar comment about losing the race flashing the desktop through the hole. The #inv-chrome host div creation + body-append lifecycle is likewise duplicated (LiveScreen.tsx:496-510 vs StreamScreen.tsx:855-877), and they have already diverged: LiveScreen's unmount cleanup calls setOverlayApiOverride(null) (LiveScreen.tsx:508) while StreamScreen sets the override in render (StreamScreen.tsx:724) and never clears it — a stale api object referencing an unmounted screen's callbacks survives until another screen overwrites it (benign today, but exactly the kind of asymmetry that turns into a bug when the next scar-fix lands in only one copy).
surviving.proposed_fix: Extract two small shared pieces in features/live: (1) an openPopout(url) helper that owns the heal-hole-then-popout_open sequence (both onPopout handlers call it, keeping their own state resets); (2) a useInvChromeHost() hook that creates the #inv-chrome div, appends/removes it, and clears the overlay-api override on teardown. One home for the race fix means the next change to the sequence can't miss a copy.

verdict.reason: Facts verify: the heal-hole popout sequence exists in both LiveScreen.tsx:454-466 and StreamScreen.tsx:664-673, and StreamScreen sets setOverlayApiOverride (line 724) without ever clearing it while LiveScreen clears on unmount (line 508). No ROADMAP/HANDOFF decision mandates the duplication. But impact is overstated on three counts: (1) the truly duplicated race-sensitive code is 3 lines, and the copies cross-reference each other (StreamScreen's comment literally says "Same open sequence Live uses"), mitigating the missed-copy risk; (2) the stale override is benign by construction — api() is consumed only by TheaterOverlay, which mounts only inside an active screen's #inv-chrome portal after that screen has re-set the override in render, so the fix is a one-line cleanup; (3) the #inv-chrome "duplicated lifecycle" claim is wrong in spirit — the two host effects diverge deliberately (StreamScreen keys on [playingUrl, popped], tears the host down while popped per its "swallowed every click" comment, and applies fullscreen state; LiveScreen is mount-once), so the proposed useInvChromeHost() extraction would share only trivial div creation and would need flags to preserve intentional differences. Real, worth a small tidy-up (shared openPopout helper + override clear), but low, not medium.

surviving.title: Shared Card/RowScroller primitives live inside the 2245-line StreamScreen.tsx; two other features import a screen file for them
surviving.evidence: Card (StreamScreen.tsx:1675) and RowScroller (StreamScreen.tsx:1292) are the shared card primitives — DiscoverScreen.tsx:8 and MyListScreen.tsx:2 both `import { Card, RowScroller } from '../stream/StreamScreen'`. So the grid features depend on a 2245-line screen module whose other 2000 lines (playback orchestration, popout, Up Next, aniskip, react-parallax-tilt import) they don't use. The StreamScreen() component itself runs 96-1193 (~1100 lines) mixing four jobs: catalog load + back/forward view history, VOD playback orchestration (resume/failover/PiP/Up Next/progress ticks — ~15 effects), the playback-stage JSX, and hosting the shared primitives.
surviving.proposed_fix: Step 1 (cheap, clearly right): move Card + RowScroller (and ContinueCard's shared bits) into features/stream/cards.tsx; update the three importers. Step 2 (optional, discuss first per the Confusion Protocol — it touches the playback stage): extract the playing-state cluster into a useVodPlayback hook + VodStage component so the screen file is navigation + rendering. Step 1 alone removes the cross-feature coupling.

verdict.reason: Line numbers, file size, and the DiscoverScreen import are all accurate, and no ROADMAP/CLAUDE/HANDOFF doc protects the current layout. But the coupling is overstated: MyListScreen is inside features/stream (not "another feature") and imports only Card — DiscoverScreen is the sole cross-feature importer. Its Card reuse is documented as intentional in MyListScreen's header comment. Also, the claim that "Step 1 alone removes the cross-feature coupling" is wrong: moving Card/RowScroller to features/stream/cards.tsx keeps DiscoverScreen depending on the stream feature; it only stops importers from pulling the 2245-line screen module, which costs nothing at runtime anyway (App.tsx eagerly imports StreamScreen, no lazy loading, single-bundle desktop app). The genuine residue — shared primitives hosted in a huge screen file and an ~1100-line component mixing catalog nav with playback orchestration — is a real but low-impact code-health tidy-up, with the larger extraction correctly gated behind discussion.

surviving.title: Credential scrubber (scrubbedMessage) and colon-preserving encSegment have no direct unit tests despite being load-bearing
surviving.evidence: scrubbedMessage is the single chokepoint keeping Xtream/AIOStreams credentials out of on-screen errors and console logs (used by live/source.ts, stream/source.ts, aioProbe.ts, Onboarding.tsx, DiscoverScreen.tsx). It's covered only incidentally (source.vod.test.ts:166 and source.stalker.test.ts:136 assert one scrubbed string each); its own edge cases — multiple URLs in one message, an unparseable URL falling back to 'https://…', non-Error input — are untested. Similarly data/stremio.ts:132 encSegment is commented 'Load-bearing; ported verbatim' (colons in tt123:1:2 must survive path encoding) and no test asserts the colon behavior — the mocked-fetch tests only use colon-free ids.
surviving.proposed_fix: Add lib/errors.test.ts (~5 asserts: origin-only output, multi-URL message, invalid-URL fallback, non-Error input, query-credential stripping) and 2-3 asserts for encSegment/addonBase in data/data.test.ts (colon preservation, slash/space escaping, trailing-slash base trim). ~30 lines total in a repo that already runs 243 units — the cheapest regression guard on offer for a credential-leak class of bug.

verdict.reason: Every cited fact verified: lib/errors.ts has no dedicated test (only lib/time.test.ts exists); scrub coverage is exactly the two incidental asserts cited (source.vod.test.ts:166, source.stalker.test.ts:136), neither exercising the invalid-URL fallback, multi-URL, or non-Error paths; encSegment (stremio.ts:132, commented "Load-bearing; ported verbatim") has zero test coverage of colon preservation — grep for encSegment/addonBase/fetchMeta across all 33 test files returns nothing, and the stremio fetch tests use only colon-free ids (colon ids in mapper/aniskip tests never pass through URL encoding). HANDOFF.md:859 independently labels encSegment load-bearing, reinforcing the finding; no doc records a decision to skip these tests. Fix is test-only, ~30 lines, no conflict with documented workarounds. Medium is proportionate for an untested credential-leak chokepoint plus an untested load-bearing encoding invariant.

surviving.title: Dead exports in data/stalker.ts: fetchShortEpg and resetStalkerSession have zero callers, and resetStalkerSession's comment claims callers that don't exist
surviving.evidence: fetchShortEpg (stalker.ts:395-412, the documented 'lazy fallback when a portal ignores get_epg_info') is referenced nowhere — not in source.ts, not in any test. resetStalkerSession (stalker.ts:59-61) is likewise uncalled, and its doc comment says '(tests, or a Settings credential edit)' — neither exists (grep across src and *.test.ts returns only the definitions). This is not on the intentional-dormant list (that covers mpv_blur/mpv_snapshot/catch-up in stream.ts); ROADMAP's Stalker entry (item 3, 'get_epg_info keyed-map EPG') never mentions a wired short-EPG fallback. HANDOFF's own lesson ('comments contradicting code, this repo's twice-burned failure mode') makes the stale resetStalkerSession comment worth fixing either way.
surviving.proposed_fix: Decide per function: fetchShortEpg — either promote to ROADMAP (wire it as the EPG fallback when get_epg_info returns empty, plausible since Stalker is 'NOT yet proven against a real portal') or delete it; resetStalkerSession — either call it from the Settings playlist-edit path (a stale session self-heals via withSession's retry today, so this is optional) or delete it and its comment. Don't leave the comment claiming callers that aren't there.

verdict.reason: Repo-wide grep confirms both exports have zero callers: fetchShortEpg (stalker.ts:395) and resetStalkerSession (stalker.ts:59) appear only at their definitions; the only data/stalker importers are Onboarding.tsx (discoverEndpoint), stream.ts (createLink), and source.ts (fetchChannels/fetchEpg/fetchGenres). No dynamic use: verify-stalker.mjs never references them (fake-stalker.mjs:240 serves a get_short_epg endpoint, but nothing calls it), and neither test file touches resetStalkerSession, so its comment "(tests, or a Settings credential edit)" claims callers that don't exist — exactly the comments-contradicting-code failure mode HANDOFF flags. Not on the intentional-dormant list (that covers stream.ts/Rust items), and ROADMAP's shipped Stalker entry describes get_epg_info only. One caveat that doesn't refute it: docs/stalker-implementation.md (lines 282-317, 437) documents get_short_epg as the planned per-visible-channel fallback and the fixture implements the endpoint, so fetchShortEpg is plan-documented groundwork that was never wired — the finding's "promote to ROADMAP or delete" fork already handles this correctly. Low impact is accurate.

surviving.title: Two hand-rolled TTL + single-flight cache implementations (live vs stream source loaders) carry the StrictMode-race guard separately
surviving.evidence: features/live/source.ts:49-196 and features/stream/source.ts:32-129 each implement config-fingerprint key → 30-min memory cache → single-flight inflight record → claim-slot-synchronously discipline. The StrictMode double-load race is a twice-documented scar (ROADMAP v0.1.104/v0.1.106: 'the slot is claimed SYNCHRONOUSLY before the async disk probe'); live's version guards it with structure + comment, stream's version independently re-implements the same discipline (loadVod calls doLoad() synchronously before any await). Both are correct today, but the guard's rationale lives in one file's comments only. Honest trade-off: live's loader adds stage fan-in + IndexedDB hydrate/revalidate, so a full shared abstraction is NOT clearly safer — the extractable core is just the keyed single-flight join.
surviving.proposed_fix: Smallest safe move: extract a ~20-line singleFlight helper (claim record synchronously, join matching key, clear-if-current in finally) into lib/, used by both loaders; leave TTL policy, stage narration, and disk hydrate in each loader. Alternative if that feels like ceremony: add a cross-referencing comment in stream/source.ts pointing at live/source.ts's race documentation so the next editor of either knows the invariant.

verdict.reason: Verified against both files: live/source.ts:49-196 and stream/source.ts:32-129 do each implement the same config-fingerprint key + 30-min TTL + single-flight inflight record + clear-if-current-in-finally pattern, and both honor the synchronous-slot-claim invariant (stream's loadVod assigns inflight at line 114 with no prior await). The StrictMode race rationale exists in-code only in live/source.ts (lines 52-57, 159-161); stream/source.ts has no comment on the invariant despite HANDOFF.md:1070 marking it a landmine that "bit us twice" (referring generically to "source.ts"). No documented decision defends the duplication — stream's header calls itself a ported "sibling," not an intentional fork. The finder's trade-off analysis is accurate: live's refreshInBackground replaces the inflight slot mid-flight and fans in stage callbacks, so a full shared abstraction is genuinely not clearly safer, and the offered fallback (a cross-referencing comment in stream/source.ts) is zero-risk and aligns with CLAUDE.md's bias against speculative abstractions. Impact "low" is correctly calibrated — both implementations are correct today; this is a future-editor hazard, not a live bug.

refuted.title: @fontsource/unbounded is a dependency with zero imports (documented as intentional)
refuted.reason: The facts are accurate (dependency at apps/app/package.json:20, zero imports, no dist/bundle cost), but the situation is explicitly documented as intentional at apps/app/src/fonts.ts:31-32: "Unbounded stays in package.json unimported — Adam likes it; it's waiting for its theme." The finder itself concedes the documented decision holds, the cost is effectively nil, and proposes no action. An intentional-and-documented state with a "no action needed" fix is not a finding; it is noise in the report.

refuted.title: My List grid has no heading while every sibling grid titles itself
refuted.reason: The raw facts check out (populated My List at /home/user/blammytv/apps/app/src/features/stream/MyListScreen.tsx:64-79 renders the gridwrap with no media-row__title; Discover's gridwrap at DiscoverScreen.tsx:396-399 always has one), but the finding fails on two grounds. (1) Intentional and documented in the file itself: the MyListScreen doc comment (lines 12-18) says "My List: Discover's grid — JUST the grid — fed by saved titles... Deliberately minimal for now — expansion (rows, sorting, cross-device) comes later," and ROADMAP.md:508 slates "My List multi-lists" as a 1.0 gate — per-list headings arrive with that expansion. The finder cites no reason to override this documented decision. (2) The premise is wrong: the screen is not unlabeled when populated. It is reached only via the pill-rail tab "My List" (AppHeader.tsx RAIL, line 27), which stays visibly active above the grid. Discover's h3 is not a screen title — it never says "Discover"; it conveys filter/search state ("Popular"/"Movies"/genre/"Results for…"), information the tab cannot carry and which My List has no equivalent of. Adding a static "My List" h3 would duplicate the selected tab directly above it — something Discover itself never does. The "loses its label the moment it has content" line is rhetorical: the empty state's "Nothing saved yet." is a message, not a label, and the actual label (the active tab) is present in both states.
